title: If This, Then That - How Computers Make Decisions
description: Students learn conditionals, write IF/THEN/ELSE logic, and debug decision rules used in everyday technology.

roleModelOfDay.name: Katherine Johnson
roleModelOfDay.intro: NASA mathematician whose conditional calculations determined whether Apollo 11 would safely reach the Moon and return. Her equations were trusted over the computers.

sloGoals.domain: Domain A — Computational Thinking (21A) & Domain B — Digital Skills (22B)
sloGoals.detail: This lesson targets conditional logic (21A): students learn to write IF/THEN/ELSE decision rules and trace how different inputs produce different outputs. They debug branching logic and connect conditionals to real technology like recommendation systems and traffic lights.
sloGoals.why: SLO digital literacy goals connect classroom learning to the real systems students encounter daily. Understanding how machines make decisions helps students critically evaluate technology — a skill central to the Dutch curriculum’s digital literacy standards.

learningObjectives: Explain what a conditional is in plain language.
Write a basic IF/THEN/ELSE rule and chain conditions with ELSE IF.
Identify where conditional logic operates in technology used every day.
Reason about edge cases and rule order in decision systems.
prerequisites: Builds on Lesson 1 (algorithms, sequences) and Lesson 2 (loops, bugs).
materials: Three colored papers: red, blue, green
Optional sticky notes for exit reflection
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Students move from sequence and loop thinking into decision logic with IF/THEN/ELSE, Boolean true/false reasoning, and ELSE IF chains. Role model: Katherine Johnson, whose conditional calculations supported safe Apollo 11 mission decisions.

teacherInstructions.setup: Place red, blue, and green papers on the floor at the front. Keep vocabulary visible all lesson: Conditional, IF, THEN, ELSE, Boolean.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question about phone auto-screen-off and game damage triggers. Collect ideas and frame conditional rules as decision instructions.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): Human conditionals activity. Students act only when IF conditions are true, then compare with IF/THEN/ELSE behavior including default ELSE.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept intro. Teach syntax IF/THEN/ELSE, Boolean true/false, and real examples (street lights, game health, notifications). Connect to sequence and loops from previous lessons.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Floor is lava challenge. Build class rules for red/blue/green sensors, execute with narrator, discuss state/order conflicts, then pairs extend with additional conditions.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on missing rules, large condition sets, and edge cases in automatic systems.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (write conditionals) and Exercise 2 (fix broken smart home). Use extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share one rule, class proposes edge cases, and complete sentence: 'A conditional is like a _____ because _____. '
discussionPrompts: Who decides game behavior when rules conflict: computer or programmer?
What changes when many options require ELSE IF chains instead of one IF/ELSE?
What edge cases might break automatic doors or school heating systems?
How do sequence, loops, and conditionals combine in one real program?
tips: Acknowledge sensor answers, then stress that rules decide actions from sensor data.
Use student mistakes in warm-up as examples of wrong branch execution.
Spend two clear minutes teaching ELSE IF order and first-true behavior.
If activity gets loud, switch to hand signals instead of shouting.
Push edge-case discussion; it is central for computational thinking growth.

teacherGuide: Core message: conditionals are decision rules that run based on true/false checks. Students should distinguish chained ELSE IF logic from separate IF statements, and understand that rule order changes behavior. Watch for students connecting loop behavior from Lesson 2 to conditional bugs in smart-home examples.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Write the Conditional
studentExercises.description: Choose one scenario and write full IF/THEN/ELSE logic with at least three connected conditions (IF, ELSE IF, ELSE).
instructions: Option A - Smart alarm clock: gentle music on weekdays, loud buzzer on Monday only, silence on weekends.
Option B - School canteen queue: pre-ordered meals first, then card, then cash; if meal is out of stock, student goes to back of queue.
Option C - Game power-up: red items add 10 points, gold items double current score, grey items do nothing, black items end the game immediately.
Write one connected IF/ELSE IF/ELSE chain, not separate IF statements.
Swap with a partner who must find one edge case your rules do not handle, then add a fix.
successCriteria: Conditional block uses IF, ELSE IF, and ELSE in one chain.
Rules match the selected scenario exactly.
At least one added edge-case fix appears after partner review.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Fix the Broken Smart Home
studentExercises.description: Identify bugs in three smart-home conditional systems and write corrected logic.
instructions: Problem 1: Heating turns on below 18 degrees but never turns off. Add ELSE IF logic to switch it off above threshold.
Problem 2: Security light turns on in daylight because darkness condition is missing. Add AND logic: motion detected AND it is dark.
Problem 3: Fridge sends 47 milk alerts per minute due to forever loop behavior. Add notified state so alerts send once until reset.
For each problem, name the bug and write a corrected conditional.
Discuss which fix depends on both loops and conditionals together.
successCriteria: All three bugs are identified correctly.
At least one fix uses combined conditions with AND.
Students explain the notification spam bug as loop + conditional interaction.

studentExercises.title: Extension - Build a Smarter Traffic Light
studentExercises.description: Design conditional rules for an adaptive traffic light using car-count sensors and emergency handling.
instructions: Write rules for green-light duration based on number of cars waiting on each road.
Add a priority rule for ambulance detection on any road.
Add behavior for 3am when no cars are waiting.
Answer challenge questions about citywide interactions between many traffic lights.
Explain one risk where optimizing one junction worsens another.
successCriteria: Rules respond to sensor data with clear conditions.
Emergency handling has explicit priority behavior.
Student identifies at least one systems-level side effect.

vocabulary.def: A rule that only runs if a certain condition is true
What happens if the condition is true
What happens if the condition is not true
A value that is either true or false — nothing in between

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these five words on the board — leave them visible all lesson.
Place one red, one blue, and one green sheet of paper on the floor at the front of the room — spaced slightly apart. These are the sensor tiles for Phase 4.
Also write the IF/THEN/ELSE syntax block on the board and leave it up all lesson: IF (condition is true) / THEN (do this) / ELSE (do this instead)
Have paper and pencils ready for the exercise phase.
lessonSlides.roleModel: Katherine Johnson (1918–2020) was a mathematician at NASA whose conditional calculations determined whether Apollo 11 would safely reach the Moon and return. She wrote the equations that told the spacecraft: IF you are on this trajectory AND within this speed range, THEN fire the engine for exactly this many seconds, ELSE abort. Without her conditionals, the Moon landing does not happen.
lessonSlides.tip: This lesson builds on Lesson 1 (sequences) and Lesson 2 (loops). At the start of Phase 3, briefly connect all three on the board: Sequence → Loop → Conditional. It takes 30 seconds and helps students see the bigger picture.
boardWords: Conditional — a rule that only runs if a certain condition is true
THEN — what happens if the condition is true
ELSE — what happens if the condition is not true
Boolean — a value that is either true or false, nothing in between

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Have you ever noticed that your phone screen turns off by itself? Or that a game character takes damage when they walk into fire? How does the device know to do that — nobody pressed a button?"
whatToDo: Ask students to turn to the person next to them and share one idea.
Take 3–4 answers from the class.
Write the most interesting ones on the board.

Then say: 'Those things happen because someone wrote a rule. Not a sequence, not a loop — a different kind of instruction. A rule that says: IF something specific happens, THEN do this. Today we are going to learn how to write those rules.'
lessonSlides.tip: Students often guess 'sensors' or 'the camera' — those are partly right, and worth acknowledging. The key point: the sensor just provides data. It is the conditional rule written by a programmer that decides what to do with that data. A sensor alone does nothing.
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Every time you walk through automatic doors at a supermarket, a sensor detects your presence — but it is a conditional that decides what to do: IF something is within 1 metre of the sensor, THEN open the doors, ELSE keep them closed. The sensor provides the data; the conditional makes the decision.

lessonSlides.title: Human Conditionals
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "I am going to read out some IF/THEN rules. If the rule applies to you, do the action. If it does not apply to you, do nothing. This is exactly what a computer does when it runs a conditional."
whatToDo: Read these rules one at a time, pausing after each one:
IF you are wearing something blue, THEN stand up.
IF you had breakfast this morning, THEN clap twice.
IF you have a pet at home, THEN wave your hand.
IF you walked to school today, THEN tap your desk.
After the fourth rule, ask: 'Did everyone do every action? Why not?'
Then say: 'What did you do when the condition was not true for you? Nothing. In coding, that nothing is the ELSE — the default when the condition is false.'
Reread the first rule with ELSE added: 'IF you are wearing something blue, THEN stand up. ELSE stay seated.' Ask: 'Is that different from what you did?' No — because doing nothing IS the ELSE.
lessonSlides.tip: If a student who has no pet waves anyway, use it: 'The condition was not true for you — that is a bug. You executed the wrong branch.' Students find this funny and it reinforces the precision concept from Lesson 1.
lessonSlides.shyStudentsNote: Some students freeze up when put on the spot. Keep the energy light — frame it as a game, not a test. If a student does the wrong action, treat it as a funny bug rather than a mistake.

lessonSlides.title: AND / OR — Combining Conditions
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "What if I changed the rule to: IF you are wearing blue OR you are wearing red, THEN stand up. Is that different from the original rule?"
whatToDo: Let students answer — more people will stand up now.
Ask: 'What if I changed OR to AND: IF you are wearing blue AND you are wearing red, THEN stand up? How many people would that be?'
Point out: 'OR means either condition is enough. AND means both conditions must be true at the same time. This idea comes up again in the exercises.'
Write OR and AND on the board next to the IF/THEN/ELSE block.
lessonSlides.tip: This is a short follow-up, not a full concept lesson. Just plant the seed — students will use AND in Exercise 2. You do not need to go deeper on Boolean operators now.

lessonSlides.title: IF / THEN / ELSE — The Decision Structure
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "This is one of the most important structures in all of computing. Almost every interesting thing a computer does is built on this pattern."
whatToDo: Point to the IF/THEN/ELSE block already on the board. Explain each part:
IF is the question the computer is asking. It is always yes or no — there is no maybe. The computer checks whether something is true or false.
THEN is what happens if the answer is yes.
ELSE is what happens if the answer is no. If you do not write an ELSE, the computer does nothing when the condition is false — which is sometimes fine, but sometimes a bug.
Now write these three examples on the board in full IF/THEN/ELSE format:
Example 1 — street lights: IF the sky is dark THEN turn the lights on ELSE turn the lights off
Example 2 — game: IF the character touches water THEN reduce health by 10 ELSE keep health the same
Example 3 — phone: IF a new message arrives THEN vibrate the phone ELSE do nothing
After Example 3, ask: 'Is do nothing a good ELSE? What if you are already vibrating from a previous notification?' This shows that ELSE matters just as much as THEN.

Now connect to previous lessons — write on the board: Sequence (Lesson 1) → Loop (Lesson 2) → Conditional (Lesson 3). Say: 'A real program uses all three. A game loops forever, sequences movements, and uses conditionals when you hit something.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Ask students: 'When does your school's heating turn on in the morning?' It is a conditional: IF the temperature drops below 18 degrees, THEN turn on the heating, ELSE leave it off. The same logic runs in fridges, traffic lights, fire alarms, and aircraft autopilot systems. Conditionals are not just a coding concept — they are how the entire built world works.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Katherine Johnson wrote conditional logic like this for the Moon landing. Every IF she wrote had to be exactly right — the astronauts' lives depended on it.

lessonSlides.title: The Floor Is Lava — Setup
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "This classroom is now a video game level. The floor has sensors built into it. Each colour triggers a different rule. You are going to write the conditional logic that runs this game — and then we are going to test whether your code actually works."
whatToDo: Point to the red, blue, and green papers already on the floor.
Ask the class to vote on what each colour does — write suggestions on the board.
A typical result: Red = lose 10 health points. Blue = gain a shield. Green = freeze — speed drops to zero.
Once agreed, tell students to write all three rules on their paper in full IF/THEN/ELSE format, including what happens if you step on plain floor (no colour).
lessonSlides.tip: Write ELSE IF on the board and explain it before students start writing: 'When you have more than two options — not just true or false, but three or four possibilities — you chain your conditionals using ELSE IF. The computer checks each condition in order and runs the first one that is true. After that, it skips all the others.' This is worth two clear minutes.

lessonSlides.title: Execute the Code
whatToDo: Ask one student to be the narrator — they read the active rules aloud as you step on tiles.
Walk around the front of the room, deliberately stepping on each colour one at a time.
The class must call out what the rule triggers — and must stay silent when you step on plain floor (the ELSE).
Make a deliberate mistake: step on blue immediately after red.

Ask: 'I just lost 10 health and now I gained a shield. Do I still have the shield? Does the shield cancel the damage I already took?' Students will disagree — that is intentional. It introduces rule order and state.
lessonSlides.tip: If the class becomes too loud, ask students to respond with hand signals instead of shouting — one finger for red, two for blue, three for green, flat hand for plain floor. Keeps the physical engagement manageable.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Katherine's code ran live during spaceflight with no second chances. Yours is a test run — hers was real. That is why precision matters.

lessonSlides.title: Pairs Extend the Rules
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "The game designer wants to make the level harder. Add two new rules to your conditional block."
whatToDo: Write these two new rules on the board:
IF foot touches RED and the player is already at zero health, THEN the game ends
IF foot touches BLUE while already having a shield, THEN nothing happens — shield does not stack
Tell pairs they must decide where in the order these new rules go — before or after the existing ones — and explain why.
Walk around and ask: 'If you check the zero-health rule after the lose-10-health rule, is that too late? If health is already zero, losing 10 more should end the game — not reduce it to negative 10.'
lessonSlides.tip: The order of conditionals matters, just as the order of steps in an algorithm matters. Students who discover this on their own are making a significant connection. No props needed — if you do not have floor space, draw a 4x4 coloured grid on the board and move a drawn character through it instead.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than three rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: "In Round 2, we disagreed about whether the shield cancelled the red damage. In real game programming, who decides the answer? The computer or the programmer?" Follow-up: "What happens if the programmer forgets to decide? What does the computer do?" The answer: the computer does whatever the code says, even if that produces a nonsensical result. A missing rule is a bug.

"Think about the automatic doors from the start of the lesson. What could go wrong with the IF/THEN/ELSE rule? What edge cases did the programmer probably not think about?" Follow-up: What if two people approach from opposite sides? What if the sensor detects a trolley but no person? What if someone stands exactly in the middle? These are edge cases — situations the programmer did not anticipate.
lessonSlides.tip: Question 3 — edge cases — is the best discussion question for this lesson. Students immediately generate examples from games they play: 'In Minecraft, what if you place a block inside another block?' or 'In FIFA, what if the ball hits the post at exactly the final whistle?' Both are real edge cases. Let the discussion run — it is valuable and connects directly to Lesson 5 on AI and bias.

lessonSlides.title: Write the Conditional
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "Choose one of these three scenarios and write the full conditional logic in IF/THEN/ELSE format. You must write at least three connected conditions — IF, ELSE IF, and ELSE."
whatToDo: Write all three options on the board:
Option A — Smart alarm clock: gentle music on weekdays, loud buzzer on Monday only, silence on weekends.
Option B — School canteen queue: pre-ordered meals first, then card, then cash. If the meal is out of stock, student goes to the back.
Option C — Game power-up: red items add 10 points, gold items double current score, grey items do nothing, black items end the game immediately.
Students work individually.
When done, they swap with a partner who tries to find one edge case their rules do not handle. The original writer adds a rule to cover it.
lessonSlides.tip: Watch for students who write three separate IF statements instead of a chained ELSE IF block. The difference matters: three separate IFs all run independently, so multiple conditions could fire at once. A chained ELSE IF block stops after the first true condition. If you spot this, ask the student: 'What happens if both the first and second condition are true at the same time?' That question usually reveals the problem.

lessonSlides.title: Fix the Broken Smart Home
whatToDo: Write all three problems on the board one at a time. Students work in pairs.
Problem 1: The heating turns on when temperature drops below 18 degrees — but never turns off. Bug: no ELSE to turn it off. Fix: add ELSE IF temperature is above 20 degrees THEN turn heating off.

Problem 2: The security light turns on perfectly during the day but not at night. Bug: the condition only checks IF motion detected — it does not check darkness. Fix: IF motion detected AND it is dark THEN turn on light.

Problem 3: The fridge sends 47 milk-low notifications per minute. Bug: the conditional is inside a forever loop with no stop after the first alert. Fix: add a notified flag — IF milk is low AND notified = false THEN send notification AND set notified = true.
Ask pairs: 'Which of these three bugs involves both a loop AND a conditional?' Problem 3 — students who connect this to Lesson 2 have understood something important.
lessonSlides.tip: Problem 3 connects directly back to Lesson 2 — a loop without a stop condition causes the bug. Acknowledge it explicitly if a student spots the connection independently. It is a significant milestone.

lessonSlides.title: Build a Smarter Traffic Light
lessonSlides.whatToSay: "A standard traffic light uses a simple loop: green 30 seconds, amber 5, red 30, repeat. But a smart traffic light uses sensor data and conditionals to decide how long each light stays on."
lessonSlides.task: Design conditional logic for a smart traffic light at a busy junction. The light has four sensors — one counting cars on each road. Write rules for: how long green stays on based on car count, what happens if an ambulance is detected, and what happens at 3am when no cars are waiting.
challengeQuestions: Can a traffic light be so smart that it causes a jam on a different road while solving one here?
If your traffic light talks to the next one down the road, what new rules become possible?
Amsterdam has 2,400 traffic lights. If they all ran your algorithm, would the city flow better or worse?
lessonSlides.tip: These questions introduce systems thinking — how individual algorithms interact when many run together. This connects directly to Lesson 5 on data and Lesson 6 on designing for real problems. Do not over-explain; just ask the questions and let students work through them.

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask 2–3 students to read one conditional rule from Exercise 1 aloud — just one rule, not the whole block.
After each one, ask the class: 'Can you think of an edge case that rule does not handle?'
lessonSlides.whatToSay: After collecting responses, close with: "You now know three of the most fundamental ideas in all of computing — sequences, loops, and conditionals. Almost every program ever written uses all three. Next week we are going to look at something that confused the entire world when it first appeared, but that you are now almost ready to understand: what AI actually is, and why it is not as clever as it looks."
lessonSlides.tip: The metaphor they choose reveals how they have understood the concept. Good answers: a referee (checks rules and makes a call), a traffic light (controls what happens based on what it detects), a bouncer (decides who gets in). All correct. Write the most interesting metaphor on the board next to the exit sentences from Lessons 1 and 2 — by Week 6 there will be a small gallery of the class's own definitions in their own words.
lessonSlides.watchFor: Did students identify that three separate IFs behave differently from a chained ELSE IF block? Did anyone independently connect Problem 3 in Exercise 2 to the loop bug from Lesson 2? Did the traffic light extension prompt students to think beyond one algorithm to the system it operates in? These three observations together are a strong signal the class is building genuine computational thinking — not just following steps, but reasoning about systems.

studentChallenges.title: The Floor Is Lava - Rule Engine
studentChallenges.description: Build and test a multi-branch ELSE IF game rule system for red, blue, green, and normal floor.
materials: Red, blue, and green floor papers
instructions: Define one effect for each color sensor tile.
Write one connected IF/ELSE IF/ELSE block that includes plain floor behavior.
Test with a narrator while a player steps on tiles in different orders.
Add two extension rules: zero-health game end, and non-stacking shield behavior.
Reorder conditions if needed and explain why order matters.
successCriteria: Rule set includes all tiles and a default ELSE case.
Pairs can justify condition order with a concrete test case.
Extended rules handle health and shield edge states correctly.

studentChallenges.title: Edge Case Detective
studentChallenges.description: Find hidden edge cases in conditionals and patch them before they become bugs.
instructions: Take one conditional block from Exercise 1 or 2.
Invent at least two edge cases where the current rules fail.
Write one additional ELSE IF or ELSE rule to handle each edge case.
Swap with another pair and test whether their patch closes the gap.
Mark any remaining unhandled case for class discussion.
successCriteria: At least two genuine edge cases are identified.
Added rules are specific and testable.
Peer testing confirms at least one bug is removed.
